blurb: Airy, daisy-like blooms on ferny stems — an effortless cottage-garden annual.
seasons: Pink/white/magenta blooms midsummer–frost; dies at first hard frost
wildlife: Bees, butterflies & hoverflies; finches eat the seed

care.hardiness: Warm-season annual — killed by hard frost, so treated as an annual everywhere on the Front Range. No winter hardiness needed; direct sow or transplant after danger of frost (mid-May around Boulder).
care.sun: Full sun is essential — at least 6–8 hours of direct light daily. In too much shade plants stretch, stems flop, and flowering drops sharply.
care.soil: Thrives in average to poor, well-drained soil. Rich, heavily fertilized or amended ground produces lush leafy plants with few flowers and weak stems that flop in wind. Colorado's clay-loam soils are fine as long as drainage is decent; sandy or gravelly ground is ideal. Slightly acidic to neutral pH (6.0–7.5).
care.water: Low to moderate once established — plants bloom best in lean, drier conditions. Overwatering or excessively moist soil suppresses flowering and promotes rank growth. Water deeply once a week in summer heat; less if rains are regular. Once self-established in a drift they are largely self-sufficient under Boulder's ~15–18 in annual precipitation.
care.spacing: Space transplants or thin seedlings to 12–18 in apart; plants reach 2–4 ft tall and 1–2 ft wide. Planting a little closer (10–12 in) lets plants support one another against Front Range wind — useful on exposed sites.
care.sow: Direct sow after the last frost when soil has warmed to at least 65°F — around late May in Boulder. Rake seeds into the top inch of loose soil, firm lightly, and water in. Germination takes 7–14 days at 70–75°F. Transplants started indoors 3–4 weeks before last frost are also fine, but direct sowing into a warm bed is easier and just as reliable.
care.depth: Sow or cover seeds to about 1/8 in deep — cosmos needs light to germinate so barely cover and firm down. Thin to final spacing once seedlings reach 2–3 in.
care.bloom: Mid-July through the first hard frost on the Front Range — the later, shorter days of high summer are what trigger heavy bloom on this day-length-sensitive plant. Deadhead faithfully in early summer to keep plants tidy; let seed set from late August onward for self-sowing.
care.feeding: Do not fertilize. Even a single application of balanced fertilizer on average or decent soil produces tall, floppy, leaf-heavy plants that bloom poorly. On genuinely impoverished soils a single low-nitrogen dose at planting is the maximum.
care.maintenance: Stake the tallest varieties (over 3 ft) in exposed spots, or plant in a drift so neighbors provide mutual support. Deadhead spent blooms through midsummer to prolong the flush; stop deadheading from late August so seed heads can ripen for self-sowing and finch feeding. Cut back to 12–18 in mid-season if plants become leggy — they regrow and rebloom.
care.selfsow: A reliable and generous self-sower — allow some seed heads to ripen and shatter and cosmos will return each year in loose drifts. Seedlings are easy to recognize (ferny, thread-fine leaves) and easy to thin or transplant when small. It is not invasive and does not appear on any CO noxious weed list.
care.troubles: Remarkably trouble-free. Aphids may cluster on young shoots; knock them off with water or leave for predators. Few fungal diseases in Colorado's dry air. Powdery mildew can appear in humid, crowded conditions — good spacing and lean soil prevent it. Tall varieties need wind protection or staking, not a disease issue.
care.harvest: Allow seed heads to dry fully on the plant (they turn tan and papery) before collecting. Strip or shake seeds into a bag, dry further indoors, and store in a cool dry place through winter. Saved seed comes true to type for straight species colors.
care.companions: Native grasses, zinnias, sunflowers, Mexican sunflower (Tithonia), bachelor's button, and other lean-soil annuals. The airy foliage pairs well with solid mounding plants like salvia or Russian sage in the mid-border.
